2. Redesign the navigation so that public users see a limited set of content (e.g., contacts and public videos), while authenticated users unlock additional icons or sections for catalogue PDFs and 360 videos.  
3. Introduce a hybrid approach where basic previews or summaries of PDFs and videos are available to all, with full access granted only after user authentication.

Original_FR: All users should be able to search by keyword or product ID.
Conflicting_FR: Only employees shall be able to perform keyword or product ID searches.
Resolution:
1. Restrict keyword and product ID search functionality to employees only, and provide a separate, simplified search method (such as category browsing or a product list) for non-employee users.  
2. Implement role-based access control, allowing all users to perform basic keyword or product ID searches, but granting employees additional advanced search filters or internal data visibility.  
3. Clarify the stakeholder intent: if Requirement B is meant to restrict access to sensitive search results (e.g., internal product codes or unpublished items), limit only those specific results to employees while keeping general product searches available to all users.

Original_FR: All Users will be able to view the next product by swiping left to right .
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall display the next product only after the user double-taps the product image.
Resolution:
1. Define distinct user interaction modes, such as a "swipe mode" for quick browsing and a "tap mode" for deliberate navigation, allowing users to switch between them in settings.  
2. Combine both actions by requiring a double-tap to activate swipe navigation, ensuring intentional engagement before enabling the swipe-left-to-right feature.  
3. Prioritize one requirement based on user testing—for instance, default to swipe navigation for simplicity but allow power users to enable double-tap confirmation via an accessibility option.

Original_FR: All users will be able to view the next product by swiping left to right.
Conflicting_FR: The next product shall be displayed automatically without requiring a swipe action.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user preference setting that allows users to choose between automatic product display and manual swipe navigation.  
2. Implement a hybrid approach where products are displayed automatically after a short delay, but users can also swipe to skip ahead or go back manually.  
3. Prioritize one requirement based on user research—for example, default to automatic display for new users while retaining swipe as an optional gesture for power users.

Original_FR: Application should do the distance calculations and geolocation information for user/site and nearby sites.
Conflicting_FR: If the distance calculation feature is invoked, the system shall require the user to manually enter the latitude and longitude coordinates for both the origin and destination points.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement B to allow automatic geolocation as the primary method, with manual coordinate entry as a fallback option when automatic detection fails or is unavailable.
2. Clarify in Requirement A that the system will first attempt automatic geolocation, but if the distance calculation feature is explicitly invoked for custom points, manual entry will be required as per Requirement B.
3. Split the functionality: implement automatic distance calculations for user/site and nearby sites by default, while providing a separate "custom distance calculation" tool that requires manual coordinate input when needed.

Original_FR: At the start of each turn the product shall notify each player of his or her status.
Conflicting_FR: The Game Master account shall be permitted to view/announce player status only during the first 5 seconds after a turn starts; outside that window no viewing/announcing is allowed.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to specify that player status notifications are sent only within the first 5 seconds of each turn, aligning with the Game Master’s permitted viewing window.  
2. Adjust Requirement B to allow the Game Master to view/announce player status at any time during a turn, while keeping player notifications as per Requirement A.  
3. Clarify that Requirement A applies to automated system notifications to players, while Requirement B governs manual Game Master actions, ensuring both can coexist without overlap.

Original_FR: Both IMS capable devices with or without UICC and non-IMS capable devices without UICC shall be supported.
Conflicting_FR: IMS service access shall be permitted to devices with an active UICC only for a single TCP session lasting no longer than 3 seconds; any longer sessions shall be terminated.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement A's support for non-IMS capable devices without UICC does not imply IMS service access; only IMS-capable devices with an active UICC may establish IMS sessions, aligning with Requirement B's restriction.

2. Modify Requirement B to allow IMS service access for devices with an active UICC under the 3-second session limit, while explicitly excluding non-IMS capable devices and IMS-capable devices without UICC from any IMS session initiation.

3. Introduce a conditional clause in Requirement A specifying that IMS service support is contingent upon UICC presence and session duration rules from Requirement B, thereby harmonizing both requirements.

Original_FR: Build Ajax Response to update home page with a link to nearby site based on user location
Conflicting_FR: When the user location is not available, the system shall display a default list of popular sites and update the home page with the generated Ajax Response.
Resolution:
1. Implement a conditional logic in the Ajax Response that first checks for user location availability; if available, include the link to nearby sites, otherwise default to the list of popular sites.
2. Design the home page to initially display the default list of popular sites, then dynamically update with nearby site links via Ajax only after user location is successfully obtained.
3. Add a user prompt or setting allowing manual override to choose between nearby sites based on location or the default popular sites, ensuring both requirements are satisfied based on user preference or consent.

Original_FR: Build the search engine based on the search form and sites available
Conflicting_FR: When the search form is submitted, the system shall ignore any user-input keywords and instead execute a pre-defined search for "breaking news" across all available sites.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the purpose of the search form: Determine if the form is intended for general user searches or only for triggering a predefined "breaking news" query, then update one requirement to align with the clarified intent—for example, modify Requirement A to specify that the search engine uses the form only for predefined searches, or adjust Requirement B to allow user keywords in addition to or instead of the fixed query.

2. Introduce a toggle or mode selection: Add a user-controlled option (e.g., a checkbox or button) on the search form that lets users choose between performing a custom keyword search (supporting Requirement A) and triggering the predefined "breaking news" search (supporting Requirement B), ensuring both functionalities are preserved.

3. Separate the functionalities: Implement two distinct interfaces—one as a standard search form for user-defined queries (fulfilling Requirement A) and another as a dedicated "Breaking News" button or link that triggers the predefined search (fulfilling Requirement B)—to avoid overlap and clearly address each requirement.

Original_FR: CNDs connected to the CPN and devoted to specific managed services shall be remotely manageable
Conflicting_FR: Full remote management privileges granted to CND-MGMT-ADMIN shall be limited to a single 30-second session per day; attempts outside that session are denied.
Resolution:
1. Define "remotely manageable" to allow for scheduled or time-limited management windows, aligning with the single 30-second session constraint in Requirement B.  
2. Introduce role-based access control where "CND-MGMT-ADMIN" has restricted daily access, but other administrative roles (e.g., service-specific admins) can manage CNDs outside that session.  
3. Implement an exception-handling mechanism that permits emergency remote management outside the daily session, with logging and approval workflows to maintain security oversight.

Original_FR: CNG shall perform a link layer multicast to unicast translation if the STB or media gateway are connected using customer network technologies not supporting multicast flows (e.g. powerlines wireless).
Conflicting_FR: Users holding the ORES key shall gain full clinical access only for the first 10 seconds after login, after which all access is restricted.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope and context of each requirement to ensure they are not mistakenly placed in the same system or module, as Requirement A pertains to network translation in a media delivery system, while Requirement B involves user access control in a clinical system.

2. If both requirements are intended for the same system, revise Requirement B to specify that the access restriction applies only to clinical data interfaces, exempting network-level operations like multicast translation described in Requirement A.

3. Introduce a priority rule or exception clause stating that network functionality (Requirement A) takes precedence over user access timelines (Requirement B) to maintain continuous service, ensuring multicast translation operates independently of login-based restrictions.

Original_FR: Confirmed registration Given the restaurant owner has applied for verification And has not received a confirmation e-mail after registration When the restaurant owner receives a confirmation e-mail Then the restaurant owner should be able to log in
Conflicting_FR: Verified restaurant owner status shall be granted only once per device, and any subsequent login attempts require re-verification regardless of manual approval.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that the initial login after email confirmation is considered the first verification event per device, and subsequent logins on the same device require re-verification as per Requirement B, ensuring both requirements are sequentially satisfied.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that login after email confirmation is provisional and must be followed by immediate device-based verification, aligning it with Requirement B’s security constraint.  
3. Introduce a distinction between “initial registration confirmation” (allowing first-time login) and “verified status” (persistent access), where the latter is governed by Requirement B, resolving the overlap in scope.

Original_FR: Consumers must be able to load electronic value to their cards at any participating load device regardless of the location provided that the currency that is requested is supported by the acquirer and the card issuer
Conflicting_FR: Card loading shall be permitted only for one transaction per hour per device, even for devices owned by the card issuer.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that the hourly transaction limit in Requirement B applies per cardholder per device, not per device globally, ensuring Requirement A's accessibility is maintained while enforcing security controls.

2. Introduce a tiered loading system where small-value loads are exempt from the hourly limit, allowing immediate access for essential top-ups, while larger loads trigger the restriction to comply with anti-fraud measures.

3. Specify that the hourly limit in Requirement B is a default security rule, but card issuers can define exceptions for trusted locations or verified customers, preserving flexibility without compromising the universal loading capability in Requirement A.

Original_FR: Contact with the vendors and give permission to sell their product under the site after testing the product quality.
Conflicting_FR: Product selling permissions shall be granted only during a 5-second daily approval window; outside this window, vendors must wait.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a two-step approval process where vendors can be contacted and product quality tested at any time, but final selling permissions are only granted during the 5-second daily window.  
2. Allow vendors to submit products for quality testing and pre-approval outside the window, with automated permission activation during the next available 5-second approval period.  
3. Extend the approval window to a longer but still limited duration (e.g., 1 hour) to accommodate manual contact and quality verification while maintaining controlled access.

Original_FR: Each time an alarm is requested, an alarm event shall be recorded.
Conflicting_FR: If an alarm is requested for the first time, an alarm event shall be recorded.
Resolution:
1. Merge the requirements into a single, clear statement: "An alarm event shall be recorded for every alarm request, including the first one."
2. Clarify the intent by specifying that Requirement A applies to all alarm requests, while Requirement B is a special case; then remove Requirement B if it is redundant.
3. Define "first time" precisely (e.g., per session, per user, or per system lifetime) to ensure both requirements can coexist without ambiguity.

Original_FR: Each time an alarm is requested, an alarm event shall be recorded.
Conflicting_FR: The alarm event shall be recorded only once per unique alarm type, and within 2 seconds of the first request.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "alarm is requested" in Requirement A to specify whether it refers to each individual request or only the first request per unique alarm type within a given time window, aligning with Requirement B's intent.

2. Modify Requirement A to state: "For each unique alarm type, an alarm event shall be recorded upon the first request, and subsequent requests of the same type within 2 seconds shall not trigger additional recordings," thereby merging both requirements.

3. Introduce a system rule that logs all alarm requests for audit purposes (per Requirement A) but only triggers a recorded "alarm event" for operational use once per unique type within 2 seconds (per Requirement B), distinguishing between request logs and event records.

Original_FR: Filling in menu field Given the restaurant owner wants to fill in the menu field in the form When the restaurant owner provides dish name And dish description And dish price Then the restaurant owner has filled in the menu field in the form
Conflicting_FR: When the restaurant owner provides dish name, dish price, and dish category, the system shall validate the price against the predefined category price range and populate the dish description automatically.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the data entry process by making the dish description optional when a valid dish category is selected, allowing the system to auto-populate it based on the category, while still permitting manual entry if needed.
2. Integrate both requirements by requiring dish name, price, and category first for validation, then auto-filling the description, and finally allowing the owner to review and edit the description before final submission.
3. Separate the workflow into two steps: first, require dish name, price, and category for validation and auto-description generation; second, present the auto-filled description in the menu field for confirmation or manual adjustment.

Original_FR: Filling in optional fields Given the restaurant owner of a restaurant wants to fill in optional fields in the form When the restaurant owner provides restaurant description And menu And type of restaurant And picture of restaurant And mobile phone Then the restaurant owner has filled in optional fields in the form
Conflicting_FR: The restaurant owner shall only fill in mandatory fields in the form, and any optional fields provided shall be ignored by the system.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of optional fields by distinguishing between initial registration and subsequent updates, allowing optional fields to be filled only after mandatory fields are completed.  
2. Introduce a user-controlled toggle or checkbox that explicitly enables optional field submission, ensuring the system processes them only when the owner actively chooses to do so.  
3. Redefine the requirements by making optional fields part of a separate, secondary form that can be accessed after mandatory fields are submitted, thereby preventing conflicts in the primary form.

Original_FR: Following a successful query the search feature will query the application database to display the search results. Following an unsuccessful query the search feature will display an error message that no matches were found
Conflicting_FR: Following an unsuccessful query, the search feature shall retry automatically until a match is found instead of displaying an error message.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a configurable retry limit: Allow the system to retry automatically a set number of times (e.g., 3 attempts) as per Requirement B, and if still unsuccessful, display the error message from Requirement A.  
2. Differentiate between query types: Apply automatic retries only for specific types of queries (e.g., real-time data lookups), while showing immediate error messages for others (e.g., static searches).  
3. Provide user-controlled retry options: Display the error message initially but include a "Retry automatically" button or toggle, letting users choose whether to enable persistent retries as in Requirement B.

Original_FR: For data with enough structuring, web scraping will be used to programmatically extract all the data needed and store it in our database.
Conflicting_FR: Web scraping shall not be used for extracting any data; all data must be entered manually.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope by defining "data with enough structuring" and identify specific datasets where web scraping is permissible, while mandating manual entry for sensitive or less structured data.  
2. Introduce a hybrid approach: use web scraping for initial bulk data import where appropriate, followed by manual validation and updates to ensure accuracy and compliance.  
3. Establish a governance policy requiring stakeholder approval for any web scraping use case, ensuring alignment with legal, ethical, and project-specific constraints.

Original_FR: For each class within a sequence for a cohort Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members shall be able to specify which quarter that class will be offered.
Conflicting_FR: Only Program Administrators shall be able to specify the quarter; Nursing Staff Members shall not have permission to define class quarters.
Resolution:
1. Restrict the permission for Nursing Staff Members to a "view-only" or "suggest" mode, where they can see or propose quarters but only Program Administrators can officially specify and finalize them.
2. Introduce a role-based approval workflow where Nursing Staff Members can propose a quarter, but the entry remains pending until a Program Administrator reviews and confirms it.
3. Clarify the requirements by distinguishing between "specifying" (definitively setting) and "recommending" a quarter, explicitly granting recommendation rights to Nursing Staff and specification rights exclusively to Program Administrators.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall examine the defensive grid of the defensive player to determine whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not examine the defensive grid before determining whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the timing by revising Requirement A to specify that the defensive grid is examined *after* the shot is taken but *before* the outcome is finalized, ensuring it aligns with Requirement B's intent to avoid premature examination.  
2. Merge the requirements into a single, precise statement: "The product shall examine the defensive grid of the defensive player only after a shot is taken, to determine whether it was a hit or miss."  
3. Define distinct scenarios: one where the grid is examined in real-time for validation (e.g., in a simulation mode) and another where it is not examined (e.g., in a blind-testing mode), and assign each requirement to a specific mode.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall mark the shot as a hit or miss on the offensive grid of the offensive player.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not display offensive shot results to maintain player privacy.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to store shot results as hit or miss in the offensive player's game logic without displaying them on the offensive grid, ensuring the data is used only for game state and not visible to the opponent.  
2. Redefine "display" in Requirement B to allow the offensive player to see their own grid results privately, while preventing the opponent from viewing any shot results, thus maintaining privacy between players.  
3. Implement a toggle or permission setting that lets players choose whether to share their offensive grid shot results, satisfying both requirements by providing optional visibility controlled by the player.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall notify the players whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not notify the players immediately; notifications will only be sent after the end of the turn.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the timing of "immediately" versus "end of the turn" by specifying that notifications are queued and displayed after the turn ends, satisfying both requirements by delaying the notification without removing the hit/miss information.

2. Introduce a two-stage notification system: provide immediate visual or auditory cues (e.g., a sound or icon) to indicate hit/miss without detailed information, then send the full notification after the turn ends.

3. Allow configurable settings where players can choose between immediate basic feedback or delayed full notifications, accommodating both preferences while maintaining game integrity.

Original_FR: For each turn the product shall allow the offensive player to define a shot.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall automatically define the offensive player's shot without user input.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a game mode selection where players can choose between manual shot definition (Requirement A) and automatic shot definition (Requirement B) at the start of a session.  
2. Implement a hybrid approach where the offensive player can optionally define a shot manually, but if no input is given within a time limit, the system automatically defines the shot (Requirement B as fallback).  
3. Clarify the scope by assigning Requirement A to a "practice mode" for skill development and Requirement B to a "quick play mode" for faster gameplay, ensuring they do not apply simultaneously.

Original_FR: For each turn the product shall identify one player as offensive status and one player as defensive status.
Conflicting_FR: Both players shall be assigned offensive and defensive status simultaneously.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the term "simultaneously" in Requirement B to specify whether it means both players receive both statuses at the same time, or each player receives both statuses concurrently, and align with Requirement A by defining distinct primary roles per turn (e.g., one player is primarily offensive while also having a defensive capability).  
2. Modify Requirement A to allow each player to have dual statuses (offensive and defensive) within a turn, but designate a primary active role (e.g., "offensive status") that determines turn priority or actions, while both retain baseline defensive status.  
3. Introduce a time-phased or conditional approach where players switch between offensive and defensive status within a turn, ensuring at any given moment one is offensive and one defensive, but both experience both statuses across the turn sequence.

Original_FR: For totally unstructured information, AI libraries will be used to programmatically extract and store any information possible
Conflicting_FR: For totally unstructured information, manual extraction shall be used to ensure accuracy.
Resolution:
1. Define a hybrid approach where AI libraries perform initial extraction of unstructured information, followed by manual review and validation to ensure accuracy before final storage.
2. Classify unstructured information by complexity or criticality, using AI extraction for low-risk or high-volume data and reserving manual extraction for high-stakes or highly ambiguous content.
3. Implement a feedback loop where manual corrections to AI-extracted data are used to continuously train and improve the AI models, gradually reducing the need for manual intervention over time.

Original_FR: Formula substitutionary ingredients shall be kept in persisted order
Conflicting_FR: Substitutionary ingredients may be reordered automatically by the system for optimization.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-configurable toggle that allows the system to either preserve the persisted order strictly or enable automatic reordering for optimization, with the persisted order as the default setting.  
2. Define a subset of substitutionary ingredients as "fixed" (persisted order) while allowing the system to reorder the remaining "flexible" ingredients for optimization purposes.  
3. Implement automatic reordering only as a suggestion that requires explicit user approval before any changes to the persisted order are applied.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the full text of the news articles, the first paragraphs are shown.
Resolution:
1. Define a clear hierarchy by specifying that Requirement A (first lines from titles) applies to summary views or headlines, while Requirement B (first paragraphs from full text) applies to detailed article views, ensuring each is used in distinct contexts.

2. Combine both requirements into a single rule: display the first line from the article title as a header, followed immediately by the first paragraph from the full text, providing a cohesive preview that includes both elements.

3. Introduce a user-configurable setting allowing the choice between showing the first lines from titles or the first paragraphs from full text, depending on user preference or use case.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, no preview lines shall be shown to minimize clutter.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-configurable setting that allows users to toggle the display of preview lines on or off, satisfying both requirements by providing flexibility.
2. Show preview lines only when the user hovers over or clicks on a news article title, keeping the default view uncluttered while offering details on demand.
3. Implement a condensed preview mode where only a limited number of characters (e.g., the first 20 characters) are shown, balancing information display with minimal visual clutter.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, no preview lines shall be shown.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the term "preview lines" to determine if it refers to the same concept as "first lines" in Requirement A, then align both requirements to either show or hide this content consistently.
2. Introduce a user-configurable setting that allows the system to toggle between showing or hiding the first lines from news article titles based on user preference.
3. Prioritize one requirement over the other based on stakeholder input, such as choosing to show the first lines if user experience testing indicates it improves readability, or hide them if minimalism is a higher priority.

Original_FR: From the here the user can either proceed to call a rep for an order or proceed to the online checkout process
Conflicting_FR: From here, the user shall only be allowed to proceed to online checkout; calling a rep is disabled.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the business rule: Determine whether the user should always be forced to use online checkout (making Requirement B correct) or if there are specific conditions (e.g., order type, user status) under which calling a representative should remain an option (modifying Requirement B to include conditional logic).

2. Introduce a conditional flow: Revise the requirements to state that the user can proceed to online checkout by default, but the option to call a representative is enabled only under certain predefined circumstances, such as high-value orders or technical errors.

3. Merge into a single, clear requirement: Replace both with a unified statement, e.g., "From here, the user shall proceed to online checkout; however, if the user selects 'Need assistance,' they may be connected to a representative before completing checkout."

Original_FR: From the here the user can either proceed to call a rep for an order or proceed to the online checkout process.
Conflicting_FR: From here, users shall only be allowed to call a rep; online checkout is disabled.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended user flow by consulting stakeholders to determine whether both options should be available or if the online checkout should be restricted, then update the requirements accordingly.  
2. Introduce a conditional rule, such as enabling online checkout only for certain user types, order values, or product categories, while defaulting to rep-assisted ordering for others.  
3. Split the process into two separate paths: one for users who prefer rep-assisted orders (as per Requirement B) and another for self-service checkout, with clear user guidance to choose the appropriate option.

Original_FR: Full information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user name And password And address And e-mail address And phone number And mobile number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification
Conflicting_FR: If the restaurant owner selects the registration option, the system shall create a temporary account requiring only a user name and password, and defer the collection of address, e-mail, phone number, and mobile number until the verification application is initiated.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to allow a two-step registration process: the first step collects only username and password to create a temporary account, and the second step collects the remaining details (address, email, phone, mobile) when the owner initiates verification.

2. Update Requirement B to include a clear validation rule that prevents submission of a verification application until all required information (address, email, phone, mobile) is provided, ensuring no conflict with the verification step in Requirement A.

3. Clarify the scope by splitting registration into "basic account creation" (username/password only) and "profile completion for verification," then adjust both requirements to reflect these distinct phases with explicit triggers and data requirements.

Original_FR: Full information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user name And password And address And e-mail address And phone number And mobile number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification
Conflicting_FR: The restaurant owner must provide verification before registration is allowed; unverified accounts cannot be created.
Resolution:
1. Allow registration with provisional account creation, but restrict full system access until verification is completed—this separates account creation from activation.
2. Require verification documents during the registration process, making verification a mandatory step before the registration can be submitted and finalized.
3. Implement a two-step registration: first, collect basic details to create a pending account; second, require verification before the account is activated and usable.

Original_FR: Full news report is shown after choosing the corresponding news title.
Conflicting_FR: Full news reports shall only display summaries instead of the entire content.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a two-step display: show a summary initially after selecting a news title, then provide an explicit user action (e.g., a "Read full report" button) to reveal the entire content if desired.  
2. Redefine "full news report" in Requirement A to mean a detailed summary that includes key points but not the complete article, aligning it with Requirement B's intent.  
3. Implement role-based access: allow certain user roles (e.g., editors) to view the entire content per Requirement A, while general users see only summaries as per Requirement B.

Original_FR: Full news report is shown after choosing the corresponding news title.
Conflicting_FR: Only a summary of the news report shall be displayed instead of the full content.
Resolution:
1. Implement a two-step display where clicking a news title first shows a summary, with an option (e.g., a "Read more" button) to expand into the full report.
2. Display the full report by default but include a toggle or setting that allows users to switch between viewing summaries only or full content.
3. Show the summary initially, with the full report accessible through a progressive disclosure method such as an expandable section or a separate detailed view page.

Original_FR: Functionalities related to Electronic Programming Guide and DRM management shall be implemented in STB or media gateway and not in CNG.
Conflicting_FR: Functionalities related to EPG and DRM shall be implemented exclusively in CNG, not in STB or media gateway.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the architectural role of each component (STB/media gateway vs. CNG) and assign EPG and DRM responsibilities based on functional layers (e.g., CNG for central management and updates, STB for local playback and decryption).  
2. Split the functionalities: implement core DRM licensing and EPG data aggregation in CNG, while keeping DRM enforcement and EPG rendering in STB/media gateway for performance and security.  
3. Engage stakeholders to review business and technical constraints, then prioritize one requirement over the other, or define a phased implementation where one component handles initial deployment and the other takes over later.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall include the designation of the heating or cooling unit and a flag to indicate whether that unit is to be turned on or off.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall exclude the flag indicating ON/OFF status and only include unit designation.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system's operational mode by revising Requirement A to specify that the ON/OFF flag is only required for manual override scenarios, while Requirement B applies to automated scheduling where status is inferred.
2. Merge the requirements by defining a conditional flag: include the ON/OFF flag when the request is user-initiated, but omit it when the request is system-generated based on predefined schedules or sensor inputs.
3. Consult stakeholders to determine the primary use case: if the system always requires explicit ON/OFF commands, adopt Requirement A; if unit designation alone suffices (e.g., in toggle-based systems), adopt Requirement B and update Requirement A accordingly.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall recognize the values for the requested heating or cooling unit and the ON or OFF status that is being requested. Since the interface to control the units has not been defined, the part of this process that will issue the signal is designated as TBD (to be determined).
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall not process any values until the interface is fully defined; signals shall not be issued for undefined units.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the TBD process by defining a placeholder or mock interface that allows Requirement A to proceed with recognition and internal processing, while Requirement B's restriction is satisfied by ensuring no actual signals are issued until the real interface is fully defined.  
2. Revise Requirement A to explicitly separate the recognition of values from the issuance of signals, stating that recognition can occur independently, but signal generation is deferred until the interface is defined, aligning with Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a conditional implementation phase: allow Requirement A to be implemented for unit and status recognition in a development environment, while enforcing Requirement B in production by gating signal issuance with an interface readiness check.

Original_FR: geo-location feature to be able to find a user
Conflicting_FR: The geo-location feature shall not track any user location to protect privacy.
Resolution:
1. Implement an opt-in system where users must explicitly grant permission for the geo-location feature to find them, ensuring privacy by default and tracking only when authorized.  
2. Use anonymized or aggregated location data for the "find a user" function, such as showing approximate areas without identifying specific individuals or storing personal tracking history.  
3. Restrict location tracking to specific, user-defined contexts or time periods—for example, allowing location sharing only during active sessions or within designated safe zones.

Original_FR: GPS Connection DESC: The application should be connected to the GPS device.
Conflicting_FR: The application shall function without GPS connection; GPS is optional.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the application's operational modes by specifying that it must have a "GPS-supported mode" for full functionality and a "standalone mode" that works without GPS, with clear user indication of which mode is active.

2. Define GPS as an optional enhancement rather than a requirement, stating the application shall function core features without GPS, but may offer additional features or improved accuracy when GPS is available and connected.

3. Introduce a graceful degradation approach: the application must operate fully without GPS, but if GPS is connected, it should utilize it to enhance performance (e.g., location accuracy) without making connectivity mandatory for basic operation.

Original_FR: Icons are according to the Android Icon Guidelines.
Conflicting_FR: Icons shall be customized arbitrarily and not follow Android Icon Guidelines.
Resolution:
1. Define distinct icon categories, such as core system icons that must follow Android Icon Guidelines and custom feature icons that can be arbitrarily customized, with clear usage rules for each category.

2. Adopt a hybrid approach where icons follow the Android Icon Guidelines for size, padding, and visual consistency but allow customization in color, shape details, or branding elements within those constraints.

3. Prioritize one requirement based on project goals—if compliance and user familiarity are critical, enforce the Android guidelines; if branding and uniqueness are paramount, allow customization—and formally deprecate or modify the conflicting requirement.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications only for critical events; all other events are ignored.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "critical events" and align it with the events covered in Requirement A, then modify Requirement B to specify that notifications are sent for all events, but critical events receive priority or additional handling.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable setting in the notification registration process, allowing clients to choose between receiving notifications for all events or only for critical events.  
3. Revise Requirement A to limit notifications to critical events by default, while providing an optional override for clients who explicitly request notifications for all events.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for specific events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events regardless of registration preferences.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-configurable setting that allows clients to opt-in or opt-out of receiving notifications for all events, with the default behavior aligning with either Requirement A or B based on stakeholder priority.  
2. Implement a tiered notification system where critical events are sent universally (per Requirement B) while non-critical events require prior registration (per Requirement A).  
3. Clarify stakeholder intent to determine whether registration should apply to all events or only specific ones, then revise one requirement to eliminate the contradiction—for example, by limiting Requirement B to only system-critical events.

Original_FR: If a command is issued by the VaadinUI for an UAV, the VaadinUI shall request control of that UAV.
Conflicting_FR: VaadinUI shall not request control of the UAV; commands are executed automatically by the UAV system.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the operational context by specifying distinct modes (e.g., manual command mode where Requirement A applies, and autonomous mode where Requirement B applies), and define triggers for switching between them.
2. Refine Requirement A to only apply when an explicit "take control" command is issued by the operator, while other routine commands follow Requirement B’s automatic execution without a control request.
3. Introduce a priority rule: if the UAV system is in autonomous operation, Requirement B applies; if the operator manually overrides, Requirement A applies, with clear indicators of the active mode in the VaadinUI.

Original_FR: If a flight plan is active and the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the FlightManager shall notify the UAV to continue to the originally designated waypoint.
Conflicting_FR: If the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the UAV shall remain hovering until manual override is performed.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system's authority hierarchy by specifying that the FlightManager's command to continue to a waypoint overrides the UAV's default hover behavior, ensuring Requirement A takes precedence when a flight plan is active.

2. Introduce a conditional clause in Requirement B to exclude scenarios where an active flight plan exists, thereby aligning it with Requirement A and preventing the conflict.

3. Define a new requirement that establishes a priority order: manual override is the highest priority, followed by active flight plan commands, with default hover behavior applying only when no other directives are in effect.

Original_FR: If a flight route is assigned to a UAV which is currently hovering in place, the FlightManager shall assign the route and maintain the hover in place mode.
Conflicting_FR: When a flight route is assigned, the UAV shall immediately exit hover mode and follow the assigned route.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior by specifying a condition: "If a flight route is assigned while the UAV is hovering, the UAV shall exit hover mode and follow the route, unless the route explicitly includes a hover segment at the current position."

2. Introduce a priority rule: "Requirement B takes precedence over Requirement A; upon route assignment, the UAV shall always exit hover mode and follow the route, overriding any hover maintenance instructions."

3. Merge the requirements into a single, unambiguous statement: "When a flight route is assigned, the UAV shall exit hover mode and follow the route; if the route requires hovering at the current location, the FlightManager shall include a hover waypoint as part of the route assignment."

Original_FR: If a lead is scored to the high range the system will supply the lead data and score to the enterprise system.
Conflicting_FR: High-range lead data shall not be transmitted to the enterprise system for confidentiality reasons.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to exclude the transmission of high-range lead data by specifying that only lead data *below* the high range is supplied to the enterprise system, while high-range data is handled through a secure internal process.  
2. Introduce data anonymization or masking for high-range leads before transmission, so the enterprise system receives necessary scoring information without confidential details, satisfying both requirements.  
3. Clarify the scope by defining separate workflows: high-range lead data is stored and processed only within the secure originating system, while aggregated or non-confidential metrics are shared with the enterprise system.

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question in the response.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "not understood" by establishing distinct categories, such as "partially unclear" (where an example can be provided) and "completely unintelligible" (where no example should be given), and assign each requirement to a different category.

2. Introduce a user preference setting that allows users to opt in or out of receiving example questions when the API does not understand their query, thereby satisfying both requirements based on user choice.

3. Merge the requirements by specifying that the system will only provide an example question if it can generate a relevant and helpful one; otherwise, it will respond without an example, ensuring compliance with both conditions.

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "not understood" to differentiate between a complete failure and a partial misunderstanding, allowing Requirement A to apply only when the API can still generate a helpful example, while Requirement B applies when no useful output is possible.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable setting that lets end-users or administrators choose whether example questions are provided, satisfying both requirements by making the behavior optional based on preference.  
3. Merge the requirements by specifying that the system will first attempt to rephrase or clarify the question without examples, and only provide an example if the user requests it or after a second failed attempt.

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: If LO < = T <  UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the boundary condition by specifying whether the upper bound (UO) is inclusive or exclusive, then merge the requirements into a single statement such as: "If LO ≤ T ≤ UO, then this process shall output the temperature status," or "If LO ≤ T < UO, then this process shall output the temperature status," based on stakeholder agreement.

2. Refine the requirements by splitting them into distinct cases: one for the open interval (LO ≤ T < UO) and another for the specific case when T equals UO, each with a defined output or action to avoid ambiguity.

3. Consult with stakeholders to determine the intended behavior at T = UO, then update both requirements to be consistent, possibly adding a note or constraint in the documentation to prevent future conflicts.

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: The temperature status shall be output no more than once per second, even if T fluctuates rapidly within limits.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the triggering condition for Requirement A by specifying that the temperature status output is only generated upon a state change (e.g., when T enters or exits the range LO to UO), and then apply Requirement B’s rate limit to that output.

2. Modify Requirement B to allow immediate output when T first satisfies Requirement A’s condition, but enforce the once-per-second limit only for subsequent outputs while T remains within the range.

3. Introduce a debouncing or sampling mechanism that evaluates T at fixed intervals (e.g., every second) and outputs the temperature status only if T is within LO to UO at the sampling instant, ensuring compliance with both the conditional trigger and the output frequency limit.

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: If T > LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended logical condition by consulting stakeholders to determine whether the system should trigger when temperature is below the lower limit (T < LO) or above it (T > LO), then update both requirements to match the correct threshold logic.

2. Merge the requirements into a single, unambiguous statement such as: "If T < LO or T > UO, the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded," ensuring it covers both lower and upper limit violations.

3. Define LO and UO explicitly in a glossary or data dictionary to confirm LO is the lower bound and UO is the upper bound, then adjust Requirement B to use "T > UO" instead of "UO < T" for consistency and clarity.

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall recognize temperature limit exceedance only if it persists for more than 3 consecutive seconds, preventing false alerts.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to include a time condition, such as: "If T < LO or UO < T for more than 3 consecutive seconds, the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded."
2. Clarify Requirement B by specifying that the 3-second persistence check applies only to "recognizing" the exceedance for alerting or logging, while instantaneous detection may still be used for internal monitoring or pre-alert states.
3. Introduce a tiered response system: Requirement A triggers an internal "potential exceedance" flag immediately, while Requirement B governs when to issue a formal alert or external notification after the 3-second persistence is confirmed.

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: If that thermostat requires no more heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the triggering condition by merging the requirements into a single, unambiguous statement: "If the thermostat's current state requires a change in the heating or cooling unit's operation, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on or off accordingly."

2. Define distinct operational modes or thresholds in the thermostat logic, such that Requirement A applies when the temperature is outside a set comfort range, and Requirement B applies when it returns within that range, ensuring mutually exclusive conditions.

3. Introduce a priority rule or state machine in the system specification, where one requirement takes precedence based on additional context (e.g., energy-saving mode or user override), and document the decision logic to avoid ambiguity.

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall submit the request only once per thermostat per heating/cooling cycle, and within 1 second of detecting the need.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of a "heating/cooling cycle" in Requirement B to ensure it aligns with the triggering condition in Requirement A, specifying whether a new cycle begins when the thermostat stops needing heating/cooling or after a fixed time period, to avoid repeated submissions.

2. Introduce a state-tracking mechanism that records when a request has been submitted for each thermostat, preventing Requirement A from triggering again until the current cycle ends as defined in Requirement B.

3. Adjust Requirement A to include a condition check that ensures a request is only submitted if no request has been made in the current cycle and within the 1-second window, merging the constraints of both requirements explicitly.

Original_FR: If the business rules allow this transaction is to be aggregated the PSAM must determine if it has an aggregation record for the card issuer. If the PSAM does not have an aggregation record for the card issuer a new aggregation record for the card issuer must be created
Conflicting_FR: The PSAM shall not create new aggregation records under any circumstances; missing records shall be ignored.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of Requirement B by specifying exceptions, such as allowing new aggregation records only when business rules explicitly permit aggregation and no existing record is found, thereby aligning it with Requirement A.
2. Introduce a conditional clause in Requirement A to state that if the PSAM is prohibited from creating records (per Requirement B), it must log an error or trigger an alternative process instead of creating a new aggregation record.
3. Reconcile the requirements by revising both to a unified rule: "The PSAM shall create a new aggregation record for the card issuer only if business rules allow aggregation and no record exists, unless system constraints prohibit creation, in which case the transaction shall be handled via exception logging."

Original_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC it shall support the AKA authentication method (TS 187 001 [8])
Conflicting_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support the AKA authentication method and shall use HTTP Digest instead.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the authentication method based on the CND's specific configuration or operational mode, specifying that AKA is used in one scenario (e.g., for 3G/4G networks) and HTTP Digest in another (e.g., for legacy or specific service access).
2. Revise Requirement B to state that the CND shall prioritize HTTP Digest authentication when AKA is unavailable or fails, ensuring both methods are supported but applied conditionally.
3. Update the requirements to reflect a phased approach, where AKA is the primary method per Requirement A, and HTTP Digest is a fallback or alternative only for backward compatibility as per Requirement B.

Original_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC it shall support the HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA
Conflicting_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended authentication method by consulting stakeholders to determine whether the CND should support HTTP Digest/NBA or not when lacking an ISIM/UICC, then revise one requirement accordingly.  
2. Introduce a conditional distinction, such as specifying different CND types or operational modes, where one scenario requires support and the other prohibits it.  
3. Merge the requirements by defining a fallback authentication hierarchy (e.g., "shall support either HTTP Digest or NBA only if a specified alternative method is unavailable") to eliminate direct contradiction.

Original_FR: If the DPU-SCUI FIFO is full when DPU-DPA attempts to enqueue its data product, DPU-DPA shall suspend event processing until room is available on the respective FIFO for the current data product. Incoming data may be dropped while suspended. An error shall be reported DPU-CCM.
Conflicting_FR: DPU-DPA shall continue processing events even if the FIFO is full, potentially overwriting incoming data.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system's priority between data integrity and continuous processing by specifying conditions: allow DPU-DPA to suspend and drop data only for non-critical events, but continue processing and overwrite data for high-priority or time-sensitive events.  
2. Introduce a configurable threshold or mode setting that lets operators choose between the two behaviors (suspend vs. continue) based on mission phase or data criticality.  
3. Redesign the FIFO to include a buffer or overflow handling mechanism that temporarily stores incoming data when full, enabling continuous processing without immediate data loss or suspension.

Original_FR: if the item the user wants is available for exchange, the user can request the exchange of items that he/she has with the item he/she wants.
Conflicting_FR: Users shall not be allowed to request exchanges even if items are available.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the context and scope by distinguishing between general user permissions and specific system states, such as allowing exchanges only during promotional events or for certain user tiers, thereby aligning Requirement A with conditional access.  
2. Introduce a role-based or tiered access model where only specific user groups (e.g., premium members) can request exchanges per Requirement A, while general users are restricted as per Requirement B.  
3. Reframe the requirements to specify different scenarios: for example, Requirement A could apply to a “wishlist exchange” feature, while Requirement B governs the standard marketplace, with clear triggers or user interfaces separating the two functions.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the high average score then it will be process by the ARA functionality.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with high average score shall be ignored by the ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "high average score" to determine if both requirements refer to the same threshold or if there are distinct categories (e.g., "high average" vs. "very high average") that would differentiate processing from ignoring.  
2. Revise Requirement A to specify that leads with a high average score are processed only under certain conditions (e.g., after manual review or if additional criteria are met), while Requirement B applies when those conditions are not satisfied.  
3. Remove or merge one of the requirements after consulting stakeholders to establish whether high-average-score leads should be processed or ignored, ensuring a single, consistent rule for the ARA functionality.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the low average then it will be returned to the supplying vendor
Conflicting_FR: Leads with low average score shall instead be processed by ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended workflow by specifying that leads with a low average score are first processed by the ARA functionality, and only those still deemed unsuitable after that processing are returned to the supplying vendor.
2. Define distinct categories within "low average score" (e.g., "low average—recoverable" vs. "low average—unrecoverable") and assign Requirement B to the first category and Requirement A to the second.
3. Revise Requirement A to apply only to leads with a low average score that have already undergone ARA processing but still fail to meet a minimum quality threshold.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the medium average then it will be set for manual verification by an Enrollment Coordinator through the eleads system.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with medium average score shall be automatically approved without verification.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "medium average score" to determine if both requirements refer to the same scoring threshold, and if not, adjust the criteria so that one range triggers manual verification and another triggers automatic approval.  
2. Introduce a decision rule: leads with a medium average score are automatically approved unless they meet specific risk-based exceptions (e.g., incomplete data, high-value product), in which case they are flagged for manual verification.  
3. Prioritize one requirement over the other based on business goals—if compliance or risk avoidance is critical, enforce manual verification; if speed and scalability are priorities, implement automatic approval.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Additional cooling requests shall be rejected instead of being queued.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system's operational mode: Specify that Requirement A (LIFO queue) applies during normal operations for load management, while Requirement B (reject requests) is enforced during emergency or maintenance states to prevent system overload.
2. Define capacity thresholds: Implement a hybrid approach where additional cooling requests are queued (LIFO) until a secondary, higher capacity limit is reached, beyond which further requests are automatically rejected as per Requirement B.
3. Prioritize based on request source: Allow queuing (Requirement A) for high-priority users or critical systems, while immediately rejecting (Requirement B) low-priority or non-essential requests to balance resource allocation and system integrity.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Requests shall be queued only if the maximum number of units is not exceeded.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "maximum number of units" to specify whether it refers to the capacity currently in operation or the total capacity including queued requests, and adjust Requirement B to explicitly allow queuing when operational capacity is reached.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that requests are only queued when the maximum operational units are running, and update Requirement B to state that queuing occurs only after the maximum operational limit is reached, not before.  
3. Introduce a priority mechanism where urgent requests bypass the LIFO queue, and revise both requirements to include exceptions for high-priority scenarios to prevent indefinite queuing of critical requests.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: If the maximum number of  cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Resolution:
1. Merge the requirements by specifying a unified rule: "If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue," and clarify that this applies to both heating and cooling systems collectively, not separately.
2. Clarify the scope by distinguishing between heating and cooling units: define separate maximum limits and LIFO queues for heating and cooling, ensuring Requirement A handles heating and cooling combined, while Requirement B handles cooling only.
3. Prioritize the requirements by deciding whether cooling units should follow a separate rule or be included under the general heating/cooling rule, then update the conflicting requirement to align with the chosen priority.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: The LIFO queue shall allow no more than 5 pending requests, rejecting additional requests until previous ones are processed.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the behavior when the queue is full: Specify whether a new request should be rejected outright (as per Requirement B) or if it should replace the oldest pending request in the LIFO queue to maintain a maximum of five items, aligning both requirements.

2. Modify Requirement A to include a conditional check: Update it to state that requests are added to the LIFO queue only if the queue has fewer than five pending requests; otherwise, follow Requirement B’s rejection rule, ensuring consistency.

3. Introduce a hybrid approach: Allow the LIFO queue to hold up to five requests normally, but if a sixth request arrives, it can either be rejected or trigger an immediate review to process the oldest request first, thus preventing queue overflow while maintaining LIFO order for the most recent requests.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is not running, this process will generate a request to turn on the requested unit.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall never generate a request to turn on a unit, even if the maximum is not reached.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "maximum number" and "requested unit" to determine if Requirement A refers to a specific scenario where an exception applies, and adjust Requirement B to allow for that exception.  
2. Introduce a priority rule, such as making Requirement B the default but allowing Requirement A to override it under predefined conditions (e.g., during emergency or maintenance modes).  
3. Merge the requirements by specifying that the system may generate a request only if both a safety check and a capacity threshold (different from "maximum") are met, thereby eliminating the direct contradiction.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall select all events and ignore any limit on the number of events in the report.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-configurable parameter or toggle that allows the operator to choose between generating a report with "selected events" (as per Requirement A) or "all events" (as per Requirement B), with the default behavior clearly specified.

2. Clarify the scope by defining distinct report types: one standard "Operational History Summary" (limited, sorted events per Requirement A) and one detailed "Full Event Log" (all events, unsorted if needed, per Requirement B).

3. Merge the requirements by having the system first select all events (satisfying Requirement B), then apply a configurable filter or limit—such as date range or priority—before sorting and generating the report (aligning with Requirement A’s sorting and file creation steps).

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope by specifying criteria for event selection, such as "select all events within a given date range" or "select events based on a user-defined filter," to replace the ambiguous "all" versus "a few."
2. Introduce a user-configurable option that allows the operator to choose between generating a comprehensive report (all events) or a summary report (a selected subset of events).
3. Merge the requirements by defining a default behavior, such as "select all events by default, but allow the system to limit the selection to recent or high-priority events if performance or relevance constraints are specified."

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall only select a single event for the operational history report.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of the operational history report by specifying whether it should include all events or a single event, and update the conflicting requirement accordingly.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable option in THEMAS to allow selection between generating a report for all events or for a single specified event.  
3. Define distinct report types: one for a comprehensive history (all events) and another for a focused single-event summary, each with separate selection criteria.

Original_FR: If the POS device supports multiple applications or multiple transaction types an interaction between the terminal and consumer or sales agent determines the CEP application and the function to be performed (cancel last purchase transaction)
Conflicting_FR: POS device shall always use the default application regardless of user interaction.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope by specifying that Requirement B applies only to initial transaction selection, while Requirement A governs post-transaction functions like cancellations, ensuring the default is used for purchases but user interaction can select cancellation functions.  
2. Introduce a hierarchical rule where the POS defaults to the primary application for standard transactions but allows user-driven application selection for specific override functions (e.g., cancellations or refunds) as described in Requirement A.  
3. Revise Requirement B to state that the POS device uses the default application *unless* a secondary function (such as canceling a transaction) is initiated via user interaction, thereby aligning both requirements through a conditional exception.

Original_FR: If the queue is not empty, this process shall remove one request from the LIFO queue and check the current state of the thermostat for which the queued request was made.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall remove requests randomly instead of checking thermostat state.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the primary goal: Determine whether the system prioritizes immediate state checking (Requirement A) or unbiased request handling (Requirement B), then modify one requirement to align with the chosen objective—e.g., if randomness is key, revise Requirement A to remove requests randomly before state checks.

2. Introduce conditional logic: Combine both by specifying that requests are removed from the LIFO queue randomly only under certain conditions (e.g., during high load), while defaulting to sequential removal and state checking as in Requirement A.

3. Split the process into two stages: Separate request removal from state checking—first remove requests randomly per Requirement B, then apply Requirement A’s state-checking logic to the removed request, ensuring both behaviors are incorporated without conflict.

Original_FR: If the settings reflect a need for a heating or cooling unit to be turned on, the process shall issue a request to turn on the appropriate unit.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall not issue requests even if a unit needs to be turned on.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the conditions under which each requirement applies, such as specifying that Requirement A is for normal operation and Requirement B is for a maintenance or emergency mode.
2. Introduce a priority rule, stating that Requirement B overrides Requirement A only when a specific condition (e.g., system lockout) is active.
3. Refine Requirement A to include an exception clause, such as "unless a system override or lockout is in effect," to align with Requirement B.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a hit the product shall allow the offensive player to define a shot.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall prevent the offensive player from defining a shot even if the previous shot was a hit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the context by specifying that Requirement A applies only to a "hit" that results in a specific game state (e.g., a successful hit that grants an extra turn), while Requirement B applies when a "hit" occurs but other conditions (e.g., a penalty or rule violation) override the ability to define a shot.
2. Merge the requirements by rephrasing: "The product shall allow the offensive player to define a shot after a hit, unless specific game rules or penalties prevent it," and then define those exceptions explicitly.
3. Prioritize one requirement over the other based on game rules: if allowing a shot after a hit is the default rule, treat Requirement B as an exception case and document the conditions under which it applies (e.g., after a foul or time limit).

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a miss the product shall change the status of the defensive player to offensive
Conflicting_FR: The product shall keep the defensive player’s status unchanged even after a miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the context of a "miss" by specifying whether it refers to a shot in a particular game phase or scenario, then adjust one requirement to align with the clarified rule.  
2. Introduce a conditional rule, such as changing the defensive player’s status to offensive only after a miss in certain defined situations (e.g., during a turnover rule), otherwise keeping the status unchanged.  
3. Merge the requirements by defining distinct player roles or states, allowing a defensive player to retain a defensive designation but gain temporary offensive capabilities under specific miss-related conditions.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a miss the product shall change the status of the offensive player to defensive
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not change the offensive player’s status even after a miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "miss" to determine if it refers to a specific type of miss (e.g., a shot that goes out of bounds versus a blocked shot) and specify under which conditions the player status should or should not change.  
2. Introduce a conditional rule, such as changing the offensive player’s status to defensive only after a miss that results in a turnover, while maintaining the status if possession is retained.  
3. Prioritize the requirements by consulting stakeholders to decide whether maintaining offensive status or switching to defense aligns better with the game’s intended mechanics and overall design goals.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall present months instead of dates for statistical report selection.
Resolution:
1. Integrate both by presenting a hierarchical selection: first, the operator selects a month from a list of available months; then, the system presents a list of available dates within that chosen month for final selection.

2. Offer a configurable user preference setting that allows the operator to choose whether to view and select by months or by dates when accessing the statistical report function.

3. Default to presenting months for initial selection to simplify the interface, but include an "Advanced" or "Show Dates" option that, when selected, reveals the full list of available dates for operators who need more granularity.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present a list of available months instead of dates.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the user's intent by consulting stakeholders to determine whether operators need to select specific dates or broader monthly periods for statistical reports, then revise the requirements accordingly.
2. Introduce a user-configurable option that allows the operator to choose between viewing available dates or available months when selecting a statistical report.
3. Combine both requirements by designing the interface to first show available months, and upon selecting a month, present the available dates within that month.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the granularity of time selection by merging the requirements: "If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available time periods (e.g., months or specific dates) from which to make a selection, configurable by user role or report type."

2. Introduce a hierarchical selection process: "If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall first present the operator with a list of available months; upon selecting a month, the system shall then display a list of available dates within that month for final selection."

3. Differentiate by report subtype: "Define two statistical report subtypes—monthly summaries and daily details—where Requirement A applies to monthly reports and Requirement B applies to daily reports, ensuring each requirement governs a distinct use case."

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall present specific dates instead of months for selection.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-configurable setting allowing the operator to choose between viewing available months or specific dates for report selection.

2. Modify the system to initially present months, then after a month is selected, present specific dates within that month for final report generation.

3. Combine both by presenting a calendar interface where months are the primary view, but clicking on a month expands to show selectable dates within it.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the granularity of time selection by merging the requirements: "If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available time periods (e.g., months or specific dates) from which to make a selection, configurable by user role or report type."

2. Separate the requirements based on context: "If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall first present the operator with a list of available months; upon month selection, it shall then present a list of available dates within that month."

3. Define a default behavior with an override option: "If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall by default present the operator with a list of available months, but provide a toggle or setting to switch to a date-based selection view if finer granularity is needed."

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present a list of available dates instead of months.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the selection granularity by merging the requirements: "If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available time periods (e.g., months or dates), with the default view configurable by user role or preference."
2. Introduce a hierarchical selection approach: "If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall first present a list of available months; upon month selection, it shall then display the list of available dates within that month."
