1. Clarify the selection method by merging the requirements: allow the user to select a topic from the search result list either by clicking on it in the list or by entering its corresponding topic ID into a command line interface, depending on the user's preference or context.

2. Define distinct user roles or modes: implement a "basic" mode where selection is made directly from the list (Requirement A) and an "advanced" mode where selection requires entering the topic ID via command line (Requirement B), with clear user guidance on when each applies.

3. Prioritize and refine: decide whether direct selection (Requirement A) or command-line entry (Requirement B) is the primary interaction method based on user needs, and treat the other as an optional or secondary feature, updating the requirements accordingly to avoid ambiguity.

Original_FR: The user shall search for the preferred repair facility using vehicle vehicle location and radius in miles
Conflicting_FR: "The user shall search for the preferred repair facility using the vehicle's VIN and the make/model of the vehicle."
Resolution:
1. Combine both search methods into a single interface that allows the user to search by either location/radius or by VIN/make/model, with the option to use both filters simultaneously for more precise results.
2. Prioritize one method as the primary search (e.g., location-based for immediate service needs) and offer the VIN/make/model as a secondary or advanced filter to refine results.
3. Clarify the use case with stakeholders to determine if the requirements serve different scenarios (e.g., emergency vs. scheduled maintenance) and design separate search flows for each context.

Original_FR: This process shall first determine a known state of all the heating and cooling units by issuing a request to turn off all the units
Conflicting_FR: This process shall first determine a known state of all the heating and cooling units by issuing a request to turn off any unit
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope by merging the requirements: "This process shall first determine a known state of all the heating and cooling units by issuing a request to turn off all units simultaneously."
2. Specify conditions for selective action: "This process shall first determine a known state by issuing a request to turn off any unit that is currently active, ensuring all units are accounted for."
3. Define a sequential approach: "This process shall first determine a known state by issuing individual turn-off requests to each unit in a predefined order until all units are confirmed off."

Original_FR: This system event shall consist of a description of the event type (a request denied event), a designation of the thermostat that made the request, and a designation of the heating or cooling unit that was not turned on.
Conflicting_FR: This system event shall consist of a description of the event type (a request denied event),  and a designation of the heating or cooling unit that was not turned on.
Resolution:
1. Merge the requirements by including both data points, specifying that the event must contain a designation of the thermostat that made the request *and* a designation of the heating or cooling unit that was not turned on, ensuring clarity and completeness.

2. Clarify the scope by determining whether the thermostat designation is necessary for all denied requests or only specific scenarios; then update one requirement to reflect the agreed-upon data set consistently.

3. Adopt Requirement A as the authoritative version, since it provides more detailed information, and revise Requirement B to align with it, preventing ambiguity in event logging.

Original_FR: This system event shall consist of a description of the event type (a request denied event), a designation of the thermostat that made the request, and a designation of the heating or cooling unit that was not turned on.
Conflicting_FR: This system event shall consist of a description of the event type (a request denied event) and only the heating or cooling unit designation, omitting the thermostat information.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of the event by specifying whether the thermostat designation is needed for logging, debugging, or user notification purposes, and update the requirement accordingly.
2. Create two distinct event subtypes: one that includes both thermostat and unit designations for detailed auditing, and another that includes only the unit designation for simplified reporting.
3. Introduce a configuration parameter that allows the system to include or exclude the thermostat designation based on operational needs or user preferences.

Original_FR: This unit will receive a text string from the URL parameter. 1 This unit will identify important words in the sentence and replace them with generic representations preceded by an escape character. (example:  ??PISB  becomes  ??$building )  1This unit will output the sentence as a string, as described in R4.  1This unit will output a map of generic representations to the words they replaced.
Conflicting_FR: If the user query contains the text string as a URL-encoded form parameter in the request body, the unit shall parse the form data, extract the text string, and perform the substitution process.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the input source by specifying that the unit should first check for a URL parameter in the request path; if not present, it should then check for a URL-encoded form parameter in the request body, ensuring only one source is processed per request.
2. Merge the requirements into a single, unified requirement stating that the unit shall accept the text string from either a URL parameter or a URL-encoded form parameter, with a defined order of precedence to avoid ambiguity.
3. Add a configuration or flag to allow the system to be set to exclusively use one input method (URL parameter or form parameter) based on deployment or operational needs, preventing runtime conflicts.

Original_FR: This unit will receive as input a generic answer from the Generic Answer Construction unit
Conflicting_FR: If the generic answer is unavailable, the unit shall source a default response template from the local configuration database.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "unavailable" in Requirement B to specify whether it includes scenarios where the generic answer is not provided by the Generic Answer Construction unit, ensuring both requirements can coexist with clear precedence.
2. Modify Requirement A to include an exception clause, such as "unless the generic answer is unavailable, in which case the unit shall follow Requirement B," to explicitly link the two and prevent ambiguity.
3. Introduce a decision logic step in the unit's design to first attempt to receive the generic answer as per Requirement A, and if that fails, proceed to source the default template as per Requirement B, documenting this flow in both requirements.

Original_FR: This unit will receive as input a mapping from the Generic Question Construction unit
Conflicting_FR: This unit will receive as input a formatted query object from the Natural Language Parser unit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the input source by specifying that the unit can accept multiple input types, such as a mapping from the Generic Question Construction unit or a formatted query object from the Natural Language Parser unit, depending on the system's mode or configuration.  
2. Merge the two inputs by designing the unit to receive a standardized data structure that can encapsulate both the mapping and the formatted query object, allowing flexibility in processing.  
3. Define distinct operational scenarios where each requirement applies, such as using the mapping for one workflow and the formatted query for another, and document the conditions for each.

Original_FR: user can add the items he/she likes to his cart by touching a Add to the carta button.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall add items to the user's cart only after the user has long-pressed the item image for three seconds.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a dual-mode interaction where the user can either tap the "Add to cart" button for immediate addition or long-press the item image for three seconds as an alternative method, with both actions achieving the same result.

2. Redefine the requirements so that the "Add to cart" button is only enabled or appears after the user has long-pressed the item image for three seconds, merging both actions into a single, sequential process.

3. Allow the user to choose their preferred method in a settings menu, making one method the default while offering the other as an optional, configurable behavior.

Original_FR:
user enters his/her username or email and his/her password and logs in the
system
Conflicting_FR: The user shall log in to the system using a one-time passcode sent to their registered mobile device.
Resolution:
1. Implement a multi-factor authentication system where the user first enters their username/email and password (Requirement A), then verifies their identity with a one-time passcode sent to their mobile device (Requirement B).
2. Offer the user a choice between authentication methods: either traditional login with username/email and password (Requirement A) or passwordless login using a one-time passcode sent to their mobile device (Requirement B).
3. Use the one-time passcode method (Requirement B) as a fallback or recovery option when the user forgets their password, while keeping the standard username/email and password login (Requirement A) as the primary method.

Original_FR: User registration - Mobile application DESC: Given that a user has downloaded the mobile application, then the user should be able to register through the mobile application. The user must provide user-name, password and e-mail address. The user can choose to provide a regularly used phone number.
Conflicting_FR: "User registration - Mobile application DESC: Given that a user has downloaded the mobile application, then the user should be able to register through the mobile application. The user must provide user-name, password, and regularly used phone number. The user can choose to provide an e-mail address."
Resolution:
1. Merge the requirements by making both email and phone number optional, but requiring at least one of them to be provided for registration.
2. Clarify with stakeholders whether email or phone number is the primary required contact method, then update both requirements to reflect that as mandatory and the other as optional.
3. Implement a two-step registration where the user provides either email or phone initially, and can add the other later in their profile settings.

Original_FR: When a request to turn on a heating or cooling unit is received, the system shall determine if the request can be honored.
Conflicting_FR: When a request to turn on a heating  unit is received, the system shall determine if the request can be honored.
Resolution:
1. Merge the requirements by generalizing Requirement B to include both heating and cooling units, aligning it with Requirement A's broader scope.
2. Clarify the scope by specifying if Requirement A is intended to cover all climate control units while Requirement B addresses a specific subset, then adjust the wording to eliminate overlap.
3. Introduce a hierarchy by making Requirement B a more specific case under Requirement A, ensuring both are retained but with clear, non-conflicting roles.

Original_FR: When a request to turn on a heating or cooling unit is received, the system shall determine if the request can be honored.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall determine request feasibility within 2 seconds, and must not honor more than one request simultaneously per heating unit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the term "honor" in Requirement A to explicitly include or exclude the feasibility check from Requirement B, ensuring that honoring a request is contingent on passing the 2-second feasibility assessment and the single-request constraint.

2. Introduce a queuing mechanism that allows the system to evaluate multiple requests sequentially within the 2-second window, but only activates one request per heating unit at a time, aligning both requirements.

3. Separate the feasibility determination from the execution phase, so that the system can assess multiple requests within 2 seconds but delays honoring any until previous requests are completed, preventing simultaneous activations.

Original_FR: When a request to turn on or off a heating or cooling unit is made, Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request to carry out that request.
Conflicting_FR: When a request to turn off a heating or cooling unit is made, Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request to carry out that request
Resolution:
1. Merge the requirements by specifying that "Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request for both turning on and turning off a heating or cooling unit," removing redundancy and ensuring clarity.
2. Clarify the scope by explicitly stating that Requirement A covers both on/off actions, while Requirement B is redundant and should be removed or marked as a duplicate.
3. Refine the language to avoid ambiguity, such as rephrasing to "Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request for any valid heating or cooling unit state change (on/off)."

Original_FR: When a request to turn on or off a heating or cooling unit is made, Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request to carry out that request.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the shutdown request within 500 milliseconds, and only if the unit has been on for at least 1 second to avoid rapid toggling.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement B's "shutdown request" applies specifically to the "turn off" case in Requirement A, and define that the 500ms timing constraint and 1-second minimum on-time only apply to shutdowns, not to startup requests.

2. Amend Requirement A to explicitly separate "turn on" and "turn off" logic, ensuring that the "turn off" action respects the 1-second minimum on-time delay and the 500ms response deadline, while "turn on" actions proceed without those constraints.

3. Introduce a state variable to track unit on-time, and specify in the requirements that Generate_H/C_Request shall only process a shutdown request if the unit has been on for at least 1 second, and shall issue that shutdown within 500ms of the original off request, effectively merging the timing conditions into Requirement A's execution path.

Original_FR: When an evidence item of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence item in red.
Conflicting_FR: When an evidence item of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the external tool is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence item in red.
Resolution:
1. Distinguish the source of modification by using different colors: color evidence items red for modifications made within OPENCOSS, and a different color (e.g., orange) for modifications made in the external tool, ensuring clear visual differentiation.

2. Combine both triggers into a single requirement: color the evidence item red whenever a modification occurs, regardless of whether it originates in OPENCOSS or the external tool, simplifying the rule and eliminating the conflict.

3. Introduce a priority rule: if both modifications occur, prioritize coloring based on the most recent change or the source with higher criticality, and log the conflict for user awareness.

Original_FR: When an evidence item of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence item in red.
Conflicting_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall indicate the modification by colouring the evidence item in red within 1 second of the external tool update, and only once per modification.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the timing condition by merging both triggers: specify that the platform must color the evidence item red within 1 second of either a modification within OPENCOSS or an update from the external tool, whichever occurs first, and ensure it only triggers once per distinct modification event.

2. Separate the triggers into distinct visual indicators: use red coloring for modifications made within OPENCOSS and a different color or pattern for updates from external tools, each with its own timing rule to avoid overlap and repeated triggers.

3. Implement a debouncing mechanism: ensure the platform detects modifications from either source but applies the red color only once within a 1-second window per evidence item, preventing duplicate indications while satisfying both timing and uniqueness constraints.

Original_FR: When an evidence traceability link of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence traceability link in red.
Conflicting_FR: When an evidence traceability link of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the external tool is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence traceability link in red.
Resolution:
1. Distinguish modifications by source: Use different colors or visual indicators for changes originating in OPENCOSS (e.g., red) versus changes originating in the external tool (e.g., orange), so both requirements can be satisfied simultaneously.

2. Combine triggers into a single rule: Update the requirement to state that the link shall be colored red when *either* the OPENCOSS data *or* the external tool data is modified, simplifying the logic and eliminating the conflict.

3. Add user-configurable behavior: Allow users to choose which modification source triggers the red coloring, or to enable both triggers independently, providing flexibility based on project needs.

Original_FR: When an evidence traceability link of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence traceability link in red.
Conflicting_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall colour the evidence traceability link in red no more than once every 5 seconds, regardless of multiple modifications in the external tool.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a debouncing mechanism that triggers the red colouring only after the last modification within a 5-second window, ensuring Requirement A’s condition is met while adhering to Requirement B’s frequency limit.  
2. Separate the visual indication into two states: immediate red highlighting for modifications within OPENCOSS, and a separate visual cue (e.g., pulsing or icon) for external changes, with the latter governed by the 5-second rule.  
3. Log all modifications internally and update the red colouring in batches every 5 seconds, displaying a timestamp or tooltip to indicate when the last change occurred.

Original_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in red.
Conflicting_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in blue.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the meaning of "pending" by consulting stakeholders to determine if different sub-statuses exist (e.g., "pending review" vs. "pending approval") and assign red to one sub-status and blue to the other.
2. Agree on a single consistent color for "pending" actions by prioritizing based on user experience guidelines or existing platform conventions, then update one requirement to match.
3. Introduce a user-configurable color setting for statuses, allowing users to customize the display according to their preferences while keeping the underlying status logic unchanged.

Original_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in red.
Conflicting_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in blue.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the meaning of "pending" by consulting stakeholders to determine if there are distinct sub-states (e.g., "pending review" vs. "pending approval") and assign red to one and blue to the other.
2. Define a single consistent color for "pending" actions based on usability standards or project conventions, and update one requirement to align with the chosen color.
3. Implement a user-configurable color scheme, allowing users to customize the color for "pending" status according to their preferences or organizational guidelines.

Original_FR: When the THEMAS system detects a request for an alarm, Generate_Alarm_Data shall detect which of the two alarms are being requested.
Conflicting_FR: When the THEMAS system detects a request for an alarm, Generate_Alarm_Data shall store which of the two alarms are being requested.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the distinct responsibilities by specifying that Requirement A focuses on detection logic (e.g., parsing the request to identify the alarm type), while Requirement B focuses on persistence (e.g., writing the identified alarm type to a database or log).

2. Merge the two requirements into a single, more comprehensive requirement: "When the THEMAS system detects a request for an alarm, Generate_Alarm_Data shall detect and store which of the two alarms are being requested."

3. Introduce a sequence or dependency: Ensure that detection (Requirement A) occurs first and passes the result to a storage function (Requirement B), making the process explicit and avoiding overlap or omission.
